source_metadata.title_raw: Daniel G. Sodergren - A-20-024 - March 2, 2020 - Pleasanton

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
· 
Daniel G. Sodergren 
City Attorney 
City of Pleasanton 
PO Box 520 
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-20-024 
Dear Mr. Sodergren: 
March 2, 2020 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pleasanton City Councilmember 
Karla Brown regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Brown take part in decisions related to bicycle and pedestrian 
improvements on West Las Positas Boulevard when a portion of the road is located more than 500 
feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of Councilmember Brown's home? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, Councilmember Brown may take part in decisions related to bicycle and pedestrian 
improvements, as the decisions would not change the development potential, income producing 
potential, highest and best use, character, or market value of Councilmember Brown's property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-20-024 
Page No. 2 
In 2018, the City of Pleasanton ("City") adopted an updated Bicycle and Pedestrian Master 
Plan ("Master Plan") that contains goals, policies, and recommendations for developing and 
implementing a citywide pedestrian and bicycle network. The Master Plan identified West Las 
Positas Boulevard as an opportunity corridor and proposed a number of improvement projects. 
West Las Positas Boulevard is a primary corridor for people traveling across Pleasanton. 
A portion of West Las Positas Boulevard is located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 
feet from the property line of City Councilmember Karla Brown's home, which she owns. The 
proposed improvements identified in the master plan that are within 1,000 feet from 
Councilmember Brown's home consist primarily of enhanced bike lanes and pedestrian crossings. 
Bike lanes and pedestrian crossings currently exist at these locations already. The project proposes 
to provide protected bike lanes and raised crosswalks to enhance safety and mobility along the 
eastern segment of this corridor. The proposed improvements in this area do not include modifying 
the number or configuration of traffic lanes. According to the City's Traffic Engineer, the proposed 
improvements would not alter traffic levels in the area. In a follow-up telephone call, you indicated 
that an Environmental Impact Report was not available, as such reports are not typically produced 
for pedestrian/bicycle projects. 
In order to implement the Master Plan's recommendations for West Las Positas Boulevard, 
the City commissioned a West Las Positas Boulevard Corridor Plan ("Corridor Plan") that was 
guided by the City's Pedestrian, Bicycle and Trails Committee. The Corridor Plan contains 
concepts to improve the existing bicycle and pedestrian facilities by: (1) collecting existing data; (2) 
identifying deficiencies; (3) collecting community and stakeholder input; (4) developing concepts; 
and (5) creating an implementation and funding plan for the West Las Positas Boulevard corridor. 
The Corridor Plan identified seven major alternatives and these alternatives were presented 
to the Pedestrian, Bicycle and Trails Committee. The proposed alternatives have the common goal 
of implementing protected intersection modifications, but the approach to implementation may 
differ depending on the alternative selected. For instance, certain proposals suggest "[u]sing low-
cost treatments like signing, striping, and soft-tipped posts or other barrier materials," at least as an 
intermediary step, while other proposals suggest the use of "full concrete median buffers and 
landscaping." The Committee will forward its preferred design to the City Council for consideration 
and direction. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." "A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) Among those 
economic interests is "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).) 


File No. A-20-024 
Page No. 3 
For the decisions at issue, Councilmember Brown's real property is not explicitly involved 
in the decision. (See Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(l)-(6).) Thus, Regulation 18701(b) 
provides the standard for determining the foreseeability of a financial effect when an economic 
interest is not explicitly involved in a decision. Under that standard, "[ a] financial effect need not be 
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public 
official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real 
property not explicitly involved in the decision, other than a leasehold interest, is material whenever 
the governmental decision involves property located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet 
from the property line of the parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's: development 
potential; income producing potential; highest and best use; character by substantially altering 
traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality; or market value. 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(8).) 
Here, Councilmember Brown owns real property located between 500 and 1,000 feet from a 
portion of West Las Positas Boulevard that would be affected by the proposed improvements. The 
proposed project alternatives are essentially enhancements of features already present along West 
Las Positas Boulevard, and it does not appear that any of the proposed alternatives would affect the 
development potential, income producing potential, or highest and best use, or market value of 
Councilmember Brown's real property. It also does not appear that any of the proposed project 
alternatives would change the character of Councilmember Brown's residence by substantially 
altering traffic levels (per the City's Traffic Engineer), intensity of use, parking, view, or privacy. 
No facts have been provided regarding potential construction to implement the proposed project, 
but you have indicated that it is not expected the proposed improvement(s) would alter noise levels 
or air quality. To the extent that is accurate and the project would not change Councilmember 
Brown's property by substantially altering the surrounding noise levels or air quality, the project 
would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the property. Accordingly, the 
Act would not prohibit Councilmember Brown from taking part in decisions related to the project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Brown take part in decisions related to bicycle and pedestrian 
improvements on West Las Positas Boulevard when a portion of the road is located more than 500 
feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of Councilmember Brown's home?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Councilmember Brown may take part in decisions related to bicycle and pedestrian 
improvements, as the decisions would not change the development potential, income producing 
potential, highest and best use, character, or market value of Councilmember Brown's property.
